text: The surgeon uses Neuron-Ferret fluorescence laparoscopy to identify fluorescent lymph nodes in the pelvic sidewall. A fluorescent lymph node is visible in the sigmoid mesocolon near the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. Another fluorescent node is evident along the line of the iliac artery in the right pelvic sidewall. These nodes are highlighted for potential lymphadenectomy and analysis.

The surgeon uses a near-infrared camera, the Pinpoint System by Novodac, to visualize the injected Indocyanine Green fluorophore in a patient with very low rectal cancer. The camera captures the fluorescence in the sigmoid mesocolon and pelvic sidewall, displaying a false color combination image that overlays standard white light and near-infrared images to guide the surgeon in real time.

The surgeon performs lymphatic mapping on a second patient with very low rectal cancer, highlighting lymph nodes along the midline and pelvic sidewalls using fluorescence laparoscopy. The augmented reality view assists in identifying specific draining nodes for selective lymphadenectomy, which are outside the standard mesorectal and mesocolonic dissection planes.
The video shows the afferent lymphatic channel leading from beneath the rectal cancer site, where the fluorophore was injected, to the iliac chain lymph node, highlighted by fluorescence.

The surgeon uses the near-infrared imaging system to guide the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery during the medial to lateral approach for abdominal perineal rectal resection. Fluorescent lymph nodes along the artery are clearly visible, aiding in identifying the correct embryological plane between the parietal and visceral fascia. The apical lymph node near the artery's origin is included in the resection specimen by adjusting the level of arterial transection.
